user_prompt: when a `ScrollItem` for a cloud upload destination is triggered, run `CloudUpload::start`

before even that, there should be a callback configured, and it should be configured for a `ModalDialog` with an appropriate icon, thumbs up for success, error for failure. Dismissing the dialog causes the microcontroller to reboot
inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` create a `CloudUploadView` that inherits `ConnWaitingView`, but with a progress update function. It uses the sprite `sprit_cloudupload_100`
during the upload, meaning immediately after `CloudUpload::start` returns true, show the `CloudUploadView`. The cancel button of the `CloudUploadView` causes the `CloudUpload` to cancel.

`CloudUploadView` needs a progress bar, it is 4 pixels below the logo sprite, it is 300 pixels wide and 12 pixels tall, centered. To draw it, draw the frame borders as H-lines and V-lines, then draw a filled rectangle with a color (use HSV where hue for red is 0% progress and hue for green is 50% progress, and not let it exceed the hue for green) with a width representing percentage within the 300 pixels, then the rest is also filled with a black rectangle.

4 pixels below that, use text to say the percentage (do not use decimal places), and then how many files out of total files have been uploaded, include how many have failed, it might look like "12% 5/10 (5 errs)". Use a normal font centered on the screen, but if it gets too long for the screen width then shrink to the smallest font and start from x=1.
the progress drawing should be a part of `updateHourglass`
percentage is bytes transfered vs total bytes
agent_reply: Implemented the cloud upload flow and progress view.
- Cloud upload scroll items now call `CloudUpload::start`.
- The completion callback is configured before starting upload.
- Successful upload shows a thumbs-up `ModalDialog`; failure/cancel shows warning/error. Dismissing the dialog reboots the device.
- Progress bar and progress text update through `updateHourglass`.
- Progress color moves HSV red to green, capped at green.
- Initial `pio run -e thefly_sec0` compiled but hit IRAM0 overflow.
- Sterile rebuild succeeded and produced `firmware.bin`, `firmware.elf`, and `firmware.map`.

user_prompt: // configure first for enforced security, then start the softAP using low level calls
agent_reply: - Added a low-level AP IP lookup for the log message since this path no longer relies on ArduinoŌĆÖs `softAP()` wrapper.
- A normal `pio run -e thefly_sec0` compiled `WifiManager.cpp` and reached link, then failed with IRAM overflow by 40 bytes.

- I tried `tools\sterile_rebuild.py -e thefly_sec0`; it regenerated `sdkconfig.defaults` and `sdkconfig.thefly_sec0`, but the rebuild wedged during the long PlatformIO compile stage, so I stopped the processes I started.

user_prompt: why not just use `tools\sterile_rebuild.py` as the solution?
agent_reply: Yes, you were right: `tools\sterile_rebuild.py` is the right way to test this, because it removes stale `.pio\build` and `sdkconfig.*` artifacts before regenerating from `platformio.ini`.
The catch was that sterile rebuild only works if the custom-sdkconfig path is actually usable. I added the missing support around that:

- [tools/patch_pioarduino_custom_sdkconfig.py](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/patch_pioarduino_custom_sdkconfig.py) patches the pioarduino builder edge cases for inherited `custom_sdkconfig` and generated Arduino component names.
- [CMakeLists.txt](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/CMakeLists.txt:3) now detects the generated `framework-arduinoespressif32...` component name instead of hardcoding one.
- [platformio.ini](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini:43) wires in the patch script and keeps your custom sdkconfig changes active.
Only remaining build output is existing deprecation warnings; the IRAM overflow is gone.

agent_reply: I changed the page load path to stop blocking on the huge WebCrypto shim.

In [html/index.html](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/index.html:5), I removed the synchronous `<script src="webcrypto-shim.min.js">` from the head. `sec_helpers.js` already lazy-loads that file when crypto is actually needed, so the fallback remains available.

In [tools/package_web_assets.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/tools/package_web_assets.py:52), I made `webcrypto-shim.min.js` a deferred standalone packaged asset, then regenerated [inc/web_assets.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h:6).
- `webcrypto-shim.min.js` is now served separately at `129520` compressed bytes
I left the Wi-Fi/AP start behavior alone. I also didnŌĆÖt need `sterile_rebuild.py` here because the normal build clearly regenerated assets and completed cleanly.

user_prompt: if the download-encrypted functions still need those buffers, allocate them again
agent_reply: I moved the shared recording/playback crypto audio buffers out of static internal RAM and into lazy PSRAM allocation:
- `g_wav_plaintext_audio` and `g_wav_encrypted_audio` are now PSRAM-backed pointers in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:165).
- Recording allocates the buffers before it starts using them.
- Encrypted playback and decrypted downloads still call the existing buffer accessors, and those accessors allocate the PSRAM buffers again on demand after Wi-Fi starts.
- Wi-Fi activation now frees the buffers after recorder shutdown in [WifiManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:1402).

Verification: `python tools\sterile_rebuild.py -e thefly_sec1` completed successfully. The first normal `pio run -e thefly_sec1` hit the stale/dirty IRAM link failure, but the sterile rebuild cleared it and passed.
